heroOverview: Glossodynia — more widely known as burning mouth syndrome — is a chronic, spontaneous burning or scalding pain of the tongue, lips, or oral mucosa in the absence of any visible mucosal abnormality or identifiable local cause. The pain is often described as constant or fluctuating, persisting for months to years, and commonly worsening through the day; the paradox that drinking cold water or eating temporarily relieves it helps distinguish glossodynia from inflammations where eating provokes pain. Population studies estimate that burning mouth syndrome affects 0.7–5% of adults, with a striking predominance in postmenopausal women, in whom prevalence reaches up to 18–33% in some clinic-based series. Despite its long history as a clinical diagnosis, the underlying mechanism has only recently been clarified as a form of small-fibre neuropathy involving trigeminal sensory pathways, a finding that now guides both investigation and treatment.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects an estimated 0.7–5% of the general adult population; up to 18–33% of women in the first decade after menopause in clinic-based series
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly postmenopausal women; the female-to-male ratio is approximately 7–10:1; rare before age 30
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence at 50–70 years, coinciding with the menopause transition; mean age at diagnosis approximately 60 years
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global burden is unknown; estimated tens of millions of adults affected based on extrapolated population prevalence data

definition: Glossodynia, or burning mouth syndrome (BMS; ICD-10: K14.6), is a chronic orofacial pain disorder defined by a burning, scalding, or tingling discomfort of the oral mucosa — most frequently the tongue tip and lateral margins, followed by the lips and palate — persisting for at least four to six months in the absence of clinically evident mucosal lesions or a detected primary local cause on examination and standard investigations. The International Headache Society classifies it as a cranial neuropathy-related disorder, and accumulating evidence shows that primary BMS involves small-fibre peripheral neuropathy of trigeminal afferents and altered dopaminergic modulation of pain processing in the basal ganglia. Secondary burning mouth symptoms that can be attributed to an identified local or systemic cause — candidal infection, iron deficiency, B12 deficiency, contact allergy, poorly fitting dentures, xerostomia, or medication — are classified as secondary glossodynia and are treated by addressing the underlying cause. The distinction is clinically important because primary BMS requires neuropathic pain management, whereas secondary forms often resolve with correction of the precipitant. The condition falls under gastroenterology and oral medicine, with input from neurology and psychiatry in complex cases.

typesClassification.description: No identifiable local or systemic cause despite thorough investigation; attributed to trigeminal small-fibre neuropathy and central sensitisation. Pain is chronic, often daily, and requires neuropathic or psychological treatment strategies.

Burning mouth symptoms attributable to an identifiable cause: oral Candida infection, nutritional deficiency (iron, B12, zinc, folate), xerostomia, contact allergy to dental materials or toothpaste, poorly fitting dentures, or side effects from ACE inhibitors or antiretroviral drugs. Resolves with treatment of the underlying cause.

Pain absent on waking, building through the morning to a peak in the afternoon and evening; associated with nutritional deficiency and parafunctional habits. Approximately one-third of patients follow this pattern.
Constant daily pain from waking to sleep; the most common pattern, affecting approximately half of patients and most strongly associated with anxiety and sleep disturbance.
Symptom-free days alternating with painful days; less common and often linked to contact allergy, specific foods, or stress fluctuation.

primarySymptoms: A persistent burning, scalding, or tingling sensation on the tongue tip and lateral margins — the sites most commonly affected — that patients describe as feeling like having drunk a very hot beverage and scalded the tongue.

Pain that is typically absent or mild on waking and builds progressively through the day, often reaching maximum intensity in the late afternoon and evening, a temporal pattern that distinguishes BMS from dental pain.
Bilateral tongue burning in most patients, though unilateral presentations occur and should prompt additional investigation to exclude a structural cause.
Simultaneous or alternating burning of the lips, the hard palate, or the mucosa of the cheeks in addition to the tongue, reflecting wider trigeminal sensory involvement.

Paradoxical improvement of the burning pain when eating, drinking (particularly cold fluids), or talking — a characteristic feature that helps distinguish BMS from most other oral pain conditions in which oral intake worsens discomfort.

Altered taste — persistent bitter or metallic dysgeusia, or a general reduction in taste intensity — reported by approximately 60–70% of patients and reflecting disruption to taste afferents in the same territory as the pain.

A persistent dry-mouth sensation (xerostomia) reported by approximately 40–50% of patients despite normal or near-normal measured salivary flow rates in most, suggesting altered mucosal sensation rather than true salivary gland hypofunction.
Difficulty concentrating, sleep disruption, and fatigue related to the chronic pain burden, particularly in patients with the Type 2 continuous pain pattern.

Heightened sensitivity to oral products — toothpastes, mouthwashes, alcohol — that most people tolerate without difficulty, reflecting the lowered sensory threshold characteristic of peripheral sensitisation.

Anxiety about the oral cavity that in some patients reaches the level of a clinically significant dental anxiety or health anxiety, often focused on concerns about oral cancer that persist despite normal oral examination.
earlyWarningSigns: A mild, intermittent tingling on the tongue tip or lip margin that was previously absent and cannot be attributed to a recent dental procedure or specific food.
A persistent dry or rough feeling in the mouth not explained by reduced fluid intake, nasal breathing, or medication.
Brief episodes of dysgeusia — metallic or bitter taste — without obvious cause, preceding the development of constant taste alteration.
New onset of oral sensitivity to toothpaste flavouring or mouthwash that was previously well-tolerated.
emergencySigns: Any visible ulcer, raised white or red patch, or indurated lump on the tongue or oral mucosa that has not resolved within three weeks — these require urgent biopsy to exclude oral cancer, as malignancy can coexist with or mimic neuropathic oral pain.
Sudden onset of severe, stabbing, electric-shock type oral pain rather than a background burning — this pattern suggests trigeminal neuralgia, which has different management from BMS.
Burning pain accompanied by dysphagia, hoarseness, or visible throat or tongue swelling — symptoms that require prompt assessment to exclude obstructive or neoplastic causes.

causes.description: The best-supported biological mechanism in primary BMS. Studies using quantitative sensory testing and confocal microscopy of tongue epithelium show reduced density of intraepithelial nerve fibres and altered thermal and pain thresholds in the trigeminal territory. This peripheral neuropathy sensitises central pain pathways, establishing a self-sustaining neuropathic pain state similar to other small-fibre neuropathies.

The sharp peak of BMS in postmenopausal women implicates oestrogen withdrawal as a contributing factor. Oestrogen receptors are present in oral mucosal cells and peripheral pain afferents; declining oestrogen may reduce the threshold for pain signalling and alter mucosal trophic support, though the exact mechanism remains under investigation. The epidemiological correlation is strong: BMS is exceedingly rare before the perimenopause in women.

Neuroimaging studies show reduced dopaminergic activity in the basal ganglia of primary BMS patients, consistent with impaired descending pain inhibition. This mirrors findings in other chronic pain conditions and explains why dopaminergic agents and medications that enhance descending inhibition (such as tricyclic antidepressants and SNRIs) have clinical benefit in some patients.

Anxiety, depression, and somatisation disorders are significantly more prevalent in BMS patients than in age- and sex-matched controls — rates of anxiety and depression exceeding 50% in some series. Psychological factors are thought to lower pain thresholds through central sensitisation rather than to be the primary cause, though they substantially amplify pain perception and are an important modifiable target in treatment.

Iron, vitamin B12, folate, riboflavin, and zinc deficiencies impair mucosal cell renewal and nerve function, producing tongue burning that is clinically indistinguishable from primary BMS. These must be excluded with blood tests in every patient because they are common, treatable, and resolve completely with supplementation without need for neuropathic pain medication.

Candida albicans colonisation — including forms without visible white plaques (atrophic candidiasis) — produces tongue burning and can coexist with primary BMS, amplifying pain. Culture-positive patients who receive antifungal treatment often report significant symptom reduction even when the tongue appeared macroscopically normal, which is why tongue swab culture is part of the standard work-up.

ACE inhibitors produce oral burning as a recognised adverse effect in approximately 1–3% of users through bradykinin accumulation; this resolves on switching to an angiotensin receptor blocker. Contact allergy to sodium lauryl sulphate in toothpaste, to cinnamon or mint flavourings, or to dental materials (acrylics, nickel, mercury in amalgam) produces a secondary burning pattern that resolves on allergen removal and is confirmed by patch testing.

riskFactors.description: The most powerful risk factor for primary BMS; the condition is rare before the menopause and prevalence rises steeply in the first decade after it. Hormonal replacement has not been shown to reliably treat established BMS, though the aetiological role of oestrogen withdrawal is well-supported epidemiologically.

Women account for 85–90% of primary BMS cases; the female-to-male ratio is approximately 7–10:1 in most clinic series, reflecting the menopausal hormonal mechanism as well as possible sex differences in trigeminal nociceptive processing.

Comorbid mood and anxiety disorders are present in over 50% of patients with established BMS and likely amplify pain via central sensitisation; addressing psychological co-morbidity with CBT, antidepressants, or both is a key component of management and has been shown in trials to improve pain scores.

Deficiency of any of these nutrients impairs tongue mucosal renewal and nerve function; while they more often cause secondary glossodynia, they can also lower the threshold for pain perception in a susceptible individual. A single blood draw identifying these is simple, cheap, and reversible.

riskFactors.factor: Dry mouth from medication or Sjögren syndrome
riskFactors.description: Reduced salivary flow from anticholinergic drugs, radiotherapy to the head and neck, or Sjögren syndrome leaves oral mucosa poorly lubricated and exposed, lowering sensory thresholds. Treating xerostomia with salivary substitutes, pilocarpine, or medication review reduces oral discomfort.

Habitual tongue pressing against the teeth or palate, bruxism, and excessive chewing of gum stimulate trigeminal afferents chronically and may initiate or perpetuate peripheral sensitisation in susceptible individuals.

Many patients date the onset of BMS to a period of significant psychological stress or life change; stress activates the HPA axis and alters central pain modulation. However, BMS persists after the stressor resolves in most patients, consistent with a self-perpetuating neuropathic mechanism.

Hypervigilance toward oral sensations, often related to fear of oral cancer, is more prevalent in BMS patients and contributes to symptom amplification. Addressing these cognitions reduces pain-related disability independently of any physical treatment.

affectedDemographics: Postmenopausal women aged 50–70, the dominant demographic in all major clinical series
Men aged over 60, who represent the minority but are affected at a much lower rate
Adults with anxiety and depression, who have significantly higher BMS prevalence
Individuals with Sjögren syndrome or other conditions causing dry mouth
People with nutritional deficiencies — particularly older adults, vegans, or those with malabsorption
Patients on ACE inhibitors or multiple anticholinergic medications
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is clinical and based on the characteristic symptom profile — persistent oral burning without visible mucosal disease — combined with the exclusion of identifiable local and systemic causes. The full oral examination must specifically look for candidal infection (including erythematous forms without plaques), mucosal lesions, salivary gland abnormalities, and signs of nutritional deficiency. Blood tests are mandatory and should include a full blood count, ferritin and iron studies, serum B12, folate, fasting glucose, thyroid function, and zinc if dietary restriction warrants it. A tongue swab for Candida culture is essential. Patch testing by a dermatologist or allergist is arranged when contact allergy is suspected from the history. Salivary flow measurement rules out true xerostomia. When the examination and investigations are normal, the diagnosis of primary BMS (ICD-10: K14.6) can be made. The symptom pattern — diurnal worsening, paradoxical improvement with eating, bilateral distribution, associated dysgeusia — supports the diagnosis. Psychological screening for anxiety and depression using validated scales (GAD-7, PHQ-9) should be part of the initial evaluation because co-morbid psychological disease is both common and treatable. There is no specific biomarker for primary BMS; the diagnosis remains one of exclusion. Biopsy is not required unless an atypical lesion is present.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, ferritin, iron studies, serum B12, and folate
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes the most common and correctable secondary causes — iron, B12, and folate deficiency — that produce burning mouth symptoms clinically identical to primary BMS.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw from the arm; results available within 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Tongue swab microscopy and culture for Candida
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies oral candidal colonisation, including the erythematous form that causes tongue burning without visible white plaques; present in up to 25% of BMS patients in some series.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A moistened cotton-tipped swab is rubbed over the tongue surface; the swab is sent to the laboratory and results are available in 48–72 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for undiagnosed diabetes, which causes peripheral neuropathy and dry mouth — both contributing to secondary burning mouth symptoms.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw after at least 8 hours of fasting; HbA1c does not require fasting and reflects average glucose over the past three months.

diagnosticTests.test: Thyroid function tests and zinc level
diagnosticTests.purpose: Thyroid dysfunction affects taste and oral sensation; zinc deficiency specifically impairs taste and may cause tongue burning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; available within 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies allergic sensitivity to toothpaste ingredients (sodium lauryl sulphate, cinnamon, mint), dental materials (acrylics, metals, mercury), or foods as causes of secondary glossodynia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Allergen-impregnated patches are placed on the upper back for 48 hours, then read at 48 and 72–96 hours; performed by a dermatologist or allergist in a specialist allergy clinic.

diagnosticTests.test: Unstimulated and stimulated salivary flow measurement
diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates true salivary gland hypofunction (which needs treatment in its own right) from the subjective dry-mouth sensation commonly reported in primary BMS with normal flow.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You collect saliva passively into a graduated tube over 10–15 minutes in the clinic; normal unstimulated flow is above 0.1 mL per minute.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of primary BMS is challenging and no single modality is universally effective, reflecting the heterogeneous neurobiological mechanisms involved. Secondary glossodynia should first be excluded and treated — correcting nutritional deficiencies, eradicating Candida, replacing sensitising dental materials, or stopping the offending drug is often curative. For primary BMS the strongest evidence supports low-dose clonazepam (used as a topical rinse rather than swallowed, to avoid dependence) and cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT). Low-dose tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline or nortriptyline) and SNRIs (duloxetine) reduce neuropathic pain through descending inhibition and have supporting evidence from clinical trials and open series. Gabapentinoids (gabapentin, pregabalin) are an option when other treatments have failed. Alpha-lipoic acid — an antioxidant with neuroprotective properties — showed benefit in several randomised trials, with doses of 600 mg daily reducing pain scores by 30–40% compared to placebo. Topical capsaicin, applied to the tongue in progressively increasing concentrations to induce substance P depletion, has shown efficacy in some trials but poor tolerability limits its use. CBT targeting pain catastrophising, health anxiety, and sleep disruption has sustained benefit in randomised trials and is recommended by oral medicine guidelines alongside pharmacological treatment. Given the chronic nature of primary BMS and the lack of a definitive cure, realistic goals include reducing pain intensity by 30–50%, improving sleep, and reducing pain-related disability rather than achieving complete pain resolution.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical clonazepam rinse
medicalTreatments.description: A 1 mg tablet dissolved in water and held in the mouth for 2–3 minutes then spat out, applied three times daily; this delivers a local benzodiazepine effect on oral mucosal GABA receptors without significant systemic absorption. It is the most studied topical agent for primary BMS.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Statistically significant pain reduction versus placebo in controlled trials; approximately 50–60% of patients report meaningful improvement with three-month use.

medicalTreatments.name: Low-dose tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline, nortriptyline)
medicalTreatments.description: Given at 10–50 mg at night, well below the antidepressant dose; these drugs enhance descending pain inhibition via noradrenaline reuptake blockade and have sedating effects that improve sleep. Nortriptyline has a better tolerability profile than amitriptyline in older patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 40–60% of patients report at least a 30% reduction in pain intensity; effects begin within 2–4 weeks of effective dosing.

medicalTreatments.name: Alpha-lipoic acid
medicalTreatments.description: An antioxidant and mitochondrial cofactor administered at 600 mg daily; hypothesised to improve small-fibre neuropathy by reducing oxidative stress in trigeminal sensory nerves. Two randomised trials reported significant pain reduction over three months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction of approximately 30–40% versus placebo in two Italian randomised controlled trials; widely used given its good safety profile and availability as a supplement.

medicalTreatments.name: Duloxetine (SNRI)
medicalTreatments.description: An SNRI given at 30–60 mg daily that provides dual noradrenaline and serotonin reuptake inhibition; particularly useful when comorbid anxiety or depression requires simultaneous treatment. Starting at 30 mg for two weeks reduces early side effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Open-label series report approximately 40–55% of patients achieving clinically meaningful pain improvement; no large placebo-controlled BMS-specific trial, but extrapolated from neuropathic pain literature.

medicalTreatments.name: Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT)
medicalTreatments.description: Structured psychological therapy targeting pain catastrophising, oral health anxiety, and sleep disruption; typically delivered over 6–10 sessions, either individually or in group format. Addresses the central sensitisation component of pain rather than peripheral mechanisms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Randomised trials in BMS show significantly greater reduction in pain interference and catastrophising versus waitlist control; benefits maintained at 12-month follow-up in approximately 60% of responders.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary trigger elimination
alternativeTreatments.description: A two-to-four week elimination of common oral irritants — sodium lauryl sulphate-containing toothpaste, cinnamon and mint flavourings, acidic and spicy foods — identifies dietary contributors without the cost or delay of formal patch testing, and is a practical first step for all patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Salivary substitutes and oral moisturisers
alternativeTreatments.description: Carboxymethylcellulose or mucin-based sprays and gels provide lubrication and a mild buffering effect on the oral mucosa; they reduce the subjective dry-mouth sensation that amplifies burning in many patients and have no side effects.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR)
alternativeTreatments.description: An eight-week structured programme teaching non-judgemental awareness of pain sensations; pilot data in chronic orofacial pain suggest reduction in pain-related distress, consistent with MBSR benefit seen in other neuropathic pain conditions.

whySeeSpecialist: Primary care is appropriate for initial investigation and treatment of secondary glossodynia. Primary BMS — confirmed after negative investigations — is best managed by an oral medicine specialist, who has experience in neuropathic oral pain, the differential diagnosis, and can coordinate care with neurology, psychiatry, or clinical psychology. Referral is also important when symptoms are atypical, when there is visible mucosal change, when initial neuropathic treatments have failed, or when significant psychological comorbidity requires specialist input.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an oral medicine specialist affiliated with a dental school or teaching hospital for the most comprehensive diagnostic work-up and access to the broadest treatment options. Ask whether the practice routinely offers patch testing, salivary flow measurement, and access to clinical psychology or CBT for chronic pain. For systemic causes, a gastroenterologist experienced in mucosal disease and nutritional deficiency is appropriate. If a psychologist is not embedded in the oral medicine team, a referral to a clinical psychologist with chronic pain experience is valuable.
preventionStrategies: Check and correct nutritional deficiencies — iron, B12, folate — annually if you follow a restrictive diet, have malabsorption, or take metformin or proton-pump inhibitors.
Switch to a sodium lauryl sulphate-free toothpaste as a precautionary measure if you begin to experience oral burning, as SLS is one of the most common contact irritants.
Address anxiety and depression proactively; chronic psychological stress lowers the neuropathic pain threshold and is both a risk factor for and amplifier of BMS.
Review medication for potential culprits — ACE inhibitors, anticholinergics, and antiretrovirals — with your prescriber if oral burning begins after starting a new drug.
Practise good oral hygiene and remove dentures at night to reduce Candida colonisation.
Keep a symptom diary when new oral symptoms develop; a record of triggers, timing, and pattern significantly speeds up diagnosis.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid oral products known to irritate sensitised mucosa: alcohol-based mouthwashes, strong mint or cinnamon flavours, and whitening toothpastes with high peroxide concentrations.
Keep cold water accessible and sip regularly throughout the day; the temporary pain relief from cold fluids is a safe and reliable comfort strategy.
Establish a consistent sleep routine; sleep disruption worsens neuropathic pain perception, and improving sleep quality independently reduces pain scores.
Reduce tobacco use and caffeine, both of which are oral mucosal irritants and stimulants that can increase central pain sensitisation.
Engage in regular physical activity; aerobic exercise activates endogenous opioid and serotonergic descending pain inhibition systems and reduces anxiety.
Manage parafunctional habits such as tongue-pressing or bruxism with an occlusal splint or habit-reversal therapy if identified as a contributing factor.

recommended: Cool water, cold herbal teas, and non-acidic cool drinks that provide immediate symptom relief through the paradoxical pain-relief mechanism of BMS
Soft, non-spicy, mildly flavoured foods that minimise oral mucosal stimulation during symptomatic periods
Iron and B12-rich foods to maintain adequate nutritional status and prevent secondary deficiency glossodynia
Yoghurt and probiotic foods that support a healthy oral microbiome and reduce Candida colonisation risk
avoid: Hot beverages and foods served at high temperature, which intensify burning on already sensitised mucosa
Very acidic foods and drinks — citrus, tomatoes, vinegar — which lower oral pH and amplify mucosal irritation
Strongly flavoured items: cinnamon, mint, and spicy condiments that commonly trigger or worsen oral burning
Sodium lauryl sulphate-containing toothpastes; switch to SLS-free alternatives as a standard first-line lifestyle change

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise is encouraged and has documented benefits in chronic neuropathic pain conditions by activating endogenous pain-inhibitory systems. Walking, swimming, or cycling for 30 minutes at least five days per week is a practical target. There are no exercise restrictions specific to glossodynia. Vigorous exercise or those requiring mouth breathing can temporarily dry the oral mucosa and may transiently worsen dry-mouth symptoms; maintaining hydration before, during, and after exercise minimises this.
dailyManagement: Keep a symptom diary noting pain intensity (0–10), triggers, dietary factors, and sleep quality to identify patterns and inform treatment adjustments.
Take prescribed medications at consistent times; low-dose amitriptyline is most effective when taken at the same time each evening for adequate descending pain inhibition.
Sip cold water or suck an ice cube when pain is at its worst; this provides safe, immediate, temporary relief through the counterirritant effect on sensitised afferents.
Practise CBT exercises (thought challenging, pacing) between therapy sessions; daily practice consolidates the neural changes that reduce pain catastrophising.
Review any new supplements or foods that seem to worsen or improve symptoms; dietary interventions are cheap, safe, and often underexplored in primary BMS.
prognosis: The natural history of primary BMS is variable: approximately one-third of patients experience spontaneous remission within five years, one-third improve substantially with treatment, and one-third have persistent symptoms despite all available interventions. Remission is most likely in patients who develop BMS within a year of the menopause onset, in whom a hormonal trigger resolving with time may be relevant. Secondary glossodynia has an excellent prognosis when the cause is identified and corrected: nutritional deficiency glossodynia resolves completely within weeks to months, Candida-related burning clears with antifungals, and drug-induced forms resolve when the offending agent is stopped. For primary BMS, the goal of treatment is realistic reduction in pain severity and its impact on quality of life rather than cure. Combination treatment — addressing neuropathic pain pharmacologically, psychological co-morbidity through CBT, and oral irritants through lifestyle change — achieves meaningful improvement in approximately 60–70% of patients.
recoveryTimeline: Secondary glossodynia from nutritional deficiency responds within 4–12 weeks of supplementation, mirroring the timeline for tongue epithelial regeneration. Candida-related burning resolves within 2–3 weeks of antifungal treatment. Drug-induced glossodynia typically improves within days of stopping the responsible medication. Primary BMS treatment with clonazepam rinses or low-dose amitriptyline typically shows early benefit within 2–4 weeks, with maximum effect at 2–3 months; CBT benefit consolidates over 3–6 months of practice. About one-third of patients show spontaneous improvement over 1–5 years.
complications: Chronic sleep disruption from constant or nocturnal pain, causing fatigue, cognitive slowing, and mood deterioration that worsen the overall pain experience through central sensitisation.
Depression and anxiety developing or worsening as a consequence of prolonged uncontrolled pain, reduced quality of life, and the diagnostic uncertainty that often delays BMS diagnosis by 2–3 years.
Oral avoidance behaviour — restricting diet to soft, bland foods, avoiding social eating — that leads to nutritional inadequacy and social isolation over time.

Missed malignancy: the rare but serious consequence of attributing a persistent tongue lesion to BMS without biopsy; any visible mucosal change should be investigated regardless of a pre-existing BMS diagnosis.

Medication side effects from long-term use of tricyclic antidepressants — dry mouth (which can worsen glossodynia), constipation, urinary retention, and postural hypotension — requiring careful monitoring particularly in older adults.

supportResources.description: Evidence-based overview of BMS from the leading global professional organisation for pain research and medicine.

supportResources.name: NIDCR — Burning Mouth Syndrome
supportResources.description: Patient information from the U.S. National Institute of Dental and Craniofacial Research on burning mouth syndrome causes and management.

sources.title: Scala A et al. Update on Burning Mouth Syndrome: Overview and Patient Management. Critical Reviews in Oral Biology and Medicine, 2003
Grushka M, Epstein JB, Gorsky M. Burning Mouth Syndrome and Other Oral Sensory Disorders. Pain Research and Management, 2003
Forssell H et al. Clonazepam in Burning Mouth Syndrome: A Double-Blind Placebo-Controlled Study. European Journal of Pain, 2002

confusedWithConditions.name: Glossitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Glossitis produces visible tongue inflammation — smooth red surface, papillae loss, sometimes ulceration — that explains the pain, whereas glossodynia (BMS) causes burning with a completely normal-looking tongue; the presence or absence of visible mucosal change is the key distinguishing feature.

confusedWithConditions.name: Trigeminal neuralgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Trigeminal neuralgia produces brief, lancinating, electric-shock-like pain triggered by light touch, chewing, or airflow, whereas BMS is a continuous or diurnal burning without sharp paroxysmal episodes; both involve trigeminal afferents but through different mechanisms and require different treatments.

confusedWithConditions.name: Depression
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Depression is a major comorbidity in BMS and not its cause; treating depression improves quality of life and reduces pain catastrophising but does not eliminate the neuropathic burning of primary BMS, which persists with normal mood in most patients.

relatedConditions.name: Glossitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Glossitis and glossodynia both cause tongue burning but are distinguished by the presence or absence of visible mucosal inflammation; B12 or iron deficiency can produce both, and they occasionally coexist.

relatedConditions.name: Trigeminal neuralgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Both glossodynia and trigeminal neuralgia involve trigeminal somatosensory pathways and cause orofacial pain; they are important to distinguish because their treatments are very different.

relatedConditions.name: Depression
relatedConditions.relevance: Depression co-occurs in over 50% of patients with primary BMS and amplifies pain through central sensitisation; treatment of depression is a core component of BMS management.

faqs.question: What is glossodynia?
faqs.answer: Glossodynia, or burning mouth syndrome, is a chronic burning or scalding pain of the tongue, lips, or mouth that persists for months or years without any visible sore or redness to explain it. The pain is real but stems from altered nerve function in the tongue rather than tissue damage.

faqs.question: What causes burning mouth syndrome?
faqs.answer: Primary burning mouth syndrome is caused by small-fibre nerve damage (neuropathy) in the trigeminal sensory pathways of the tongue, combined with altered pain processing in the brain. Secondary causes — which must be excluded — include iron or B12 deficiency, oral Candida infection, dry mouth, contact allergy to toothpaste ingredients, and ACE inhibitor medication.

faqs.question: How is burning mouth syndrome diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is by clinical examination — the tongue and mouth look entirely normal — combined with blood tests to exclude nutritional deficiency, a tongue swab for Candida, and a review of medications and dental materials. When all tests are normal and the burning follows the characteristic diurnal pattern with paradoxical relief from eating, the diagnosis of primary burning mouth syndrome is confirmed.

faqs.question: Is burning mouth syndrome serious?
faqs.answer: Burning mouth syndrome itself does not damage tissue or cause cancer. However, it significantly affects quality of life, sleep, and mood when uncontrolled. Any visible ulcer or lesion in the mouth that does not heal within three weeks must be assessed urgently because oral cancer can mimic BMS, and the two diagnoses require urgent biopsy to distinguish.

faqs.question: Who gets glossodynia?
faqs.answer: Burning mouth syndrome predominantly affects postmenopausal women aged 50–70, in whom prevalence may reach up to 18% in specialist series. It is rare before age 30 and uncommon in men, though both sexes can be affected. Adults with anxiety, nutritional deficiencies, or dry mouth have a higher risk.

faqs.question: Does burning mouth syndrome go away on its own?
faqs.answer: About one-third of patients with primary burning mouth syndrome experience spontaneous improvement or remission within five years. For others it persists long-term. Secondary burning mouth from nutritional deficiency, Candida, or a medication resolves completely once the cause is treated. This is why identifying and correcting secondary causes is the most important first step.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for burning mouth syndrome?
faqs.answer: No single treatment works for everyone. The best evidence supports topical clonazepam rinse, low-dose amitriptyline at night, alpha-lipoic acid (600 mg daily), and cognitive behavioural therapy. Treating any identified secondary cause — iron deficiency, Candida, medication side effect — often resolves the problem entirely and should always come first.

faqs.question: Why does eating relieve burning mouth pain?
faqs.answer: Many BMS patients notice the burning temporarily eases or disappears while eating or drinking cold fluids. This paradoxical relief occurs because taste and temperature sensations during eating partially inhibit the abnormal pain signals generated by damaged trigeminal sensory nerve fibres, a mechanism similar to the gate-control theory of pain. Hot food or drink typically worsens pain.

faqs.question: Can nutritional deficiency cause burning tongue?
faqs.answer: Yes. Deficiencies in iron, vitamin B12, folate, riboflavin, and zinc all cause burning tongue that is clinically identical to primary BMS. This is why blood tests for these nutrients are the first investigation in any new case. Correcting the deficiency typically resolves the burning within 4–12 weeks without any neuropathic pain medication.

faqs.question: Is burning mouth syndrome related to menopause?
faqs.answer: Yes, strongly. The condition is extremely rare before the menopause and prevalence rises sharply in the first decade after it. Declining oestrogen likely alters trigeminal nerve sensitivity and oral mucosal support. However, hormone replacement therapy does not reliably cure established BMS, suggesting that hormonal change triggers rather than solely causes the neuropathic process.

faqs.question: Can toothpaste cause burning mouth?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sodium lauryl sulphate — a foaming agent in most regular toothpastes — is one of the most common contact irritants for the oral mucosa and can cause or worsen burning mouth. Switching to an SLS-free toothpaste for four weeks is a safe, cheap first step that resolves the problem in a proportion of patients, particularly those with the intermittent pain pattern.

faqs.question: Can anxiety cause burning mouth syndrome?
faqs.answer: Anxiety does not directly cause the neuropathic nerve damage underlying primary BMS, but it significantly amplifies pain through central sensitisation and lowers the threshold for discomfort. Over 50% of BMS patients have comorbid anxiety. Treating anxiety with CBT or medication improves both pain scores and quality of life, making psychological assessment an important part of every BMS evaluation.

faqs.question: Does burning mouth syndrome affect taste?
faqs.answer: Yes. Approximately 60–70% of BMS patients report altered taste (dysgeusia), often described as a persistent metallic or bitter background taste, or a general blunting of flavour perception. Taste changes occur because the same trigeminal and chorda tympani nerve fibres that carry pain also carry taste information, and their dysfunction affects both modalities simultaneously.

faqs.question: Can medications cause burning mouth syndrome?
faqs.answer: Yes. ACE inhibitors (such as lisinopril or enalapril) cause oral burning in 1–3% of users through bradykinin accumulation. Antiretroviral drugs, some chemotherapy agents, and multiple medications that cause dry mouth (antihistamines, antidepressants, antipsychotics) can all trigger or worsen mouth burning. Switching from an ACE inhibitor to an ARB often resolves drug-induced burning within weeks.

faqs.question: Is burning mouth syndrome the same as oral thrush?
faqs.answer: No, but Candida infection — including erythematous forms without visible white plaques — can cause burning mouth symptoms and must be excluded. A tongue swab culture is part of the standard BMS work-up. Oral thrush and primary BMS can coexist, and treating the Candida may substantially reduce burning even when the tongue appears normal to the naked eye.

faqs.question: How long does burning mouth syndrome last?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, primary BMS can persist for years and sometimes decades. About one-third of patients improve spontaneously within five years. With treatment — neuropathic pain medication and CBT — approximately 60–70% achieve meaningful reduction in pain over 3–6 months. Secondary burning mouth from a correctable cause resolves within days to weeks of effective treatment.

faqs.question: What foods should I avoid with burning mouth syndrome?
faqs.answer: Avoid very hot foods and drinks, which worsen sensitised nerve endings. Very acidic items — citrus juice, vinegar, tomato-based foods — lower oral pH and increase mucosal irritation. Strong flavourings such as cinnamon and mint are common triggers. Switching to an SLS-free toothpaste and avoiding alcohol-based mouthwash are among the most impactful dietary and product changes.

faqs.question: Can burning mouth syndrome be cured?
faqs.answer: Secondary burning mouth from a nutritional deficiency, Candida, or medication is effectively cured by addressing the cause. Primary BMS has no definitive cure, but approximately one-third of patients achieve spontaneous remission within five years, and treatment reduces pain meaningfully in the majority. A combination of neuropathic medication, CBT, and lifestyle changes provides the best long-term outcomes.

faqs.question: Should I see a specialist for burning tongue?
faqs.answer: See your GP or dentist first for an oral examination and basic blood tests. If investigations are normal and symptoms persist for more than a few weeks, referral to an oral medicine specialist is appropriate. An oral medicine specialist can conduct patch testing, salivary flow assessment, and prescribe neuropathic pain treatments, and can coordinate CBT or clinical psychology referral.

faqs.question: Can CBT help burning mouth syndrome?
faqs.answer: Yes. Randomised controlled trials show that cognitive behavioural therapy reduces pain intensity, pain catastrophising, and the emotional impact of BMS, with benefits maintained at 12 months. CBT addresses the central sensitisation and health anxiety that amplify neuropathic pain, complementing pharmacological treatments rather than replacing them.

metaDescription: Glossodynia causes chronic tongue burning with no visible sore — often neuropathic or nutritional. Learn symptoms, diagnosis, and treatments. Doctor-reviewed.
